Closed fracture of neck of left femur, initial encounter (CMS/HCC) [S72.002A]
Closed subcapital fracture of neck of femur, left, initial encounter (CMS/HCC)
Linda M Spear is a 74 y.o. female admitted for closed fracture of the left femoral neck who
was also found to have an acute versus subacute T11 compression fracture following a
ground level fall. Following initial evaluation by the trauma surgery service, the orthopedic
surgery team and the UMC hospitalist service were consulted for evaluation and
Spear, Linda M (MRN 0000333437) Printed by Rosalie Aquino, RN [28092] at 9/27/2025 8:27 AM
management of the left femoral neck fracture, the patient was subsequently admitted under
the G60 protocol. Upon admission to the hospital the patient underwent peripheral nerve
block with the anesthesia, the patient tolerated the procedure well with no immediate
complications. On hospital day 2 patient was evaluated by Neurosurgery for the acute to
subacute T11 compression fracture without retropulsion. Neurosurgery recommended a
TLSO brace when out of bed at all times. On hospital day 2 patient also underwent a left
partial hip arthroplasty with with the orthopedic surgery team. Patient tolerated the
procedure well with no immediate intraoperative or postoperative complications. On
hospital day 3 patient began working with physical therapy and and occupational therapy.
On the day of discharge, patient's pain was well controled, she was tolerating a regular diet
voiding spontaneously , and ambulating well with minimal/no assistance Discharge
medications and a plan for close outpatient follow up were reviewed in detail, and phone
numbers were provided both for non-urgent issues and appointment scheduling as well as
urgent questions. Pain was under control and it was decided the patient could safely be
BP (!) 148/79 I Pulse 65 I Temp 36.7 °C (98 °F) (Oral) I Resp 16 I Ht 172.7 cm (5' 8")
Wt 64.5 kg (142 lb 3.2 oz) I SpO2 98% I BMI 21.62 kg/m2
Pupils: Pupils are equal, round, and reactive to light.
Rate and Rhythm: Normal rate and regular rhythm.
Cervical back: Normal range of motion and neck supple.
General: Skin is warm and dry.
Capillary Refill: Capillary refill takes less than 2 seconds.
Mental Status: She is alert and oriented to person, place, and time.
Spear, Linda M (MRN 0000333437) Printed by Rosalie Aquino, RN [28092] at 9/27/2025 8:27 AM
TLSO brace when out of bed
at all times, ok to apply

Spear, Linda M (MRN 0000333437) Printed by Rosalie Aquino, RN [28092] at 9/27/2025 8:27 AM
Weight bearing: wear bearing as tolerated to your left lower extremity, posterior hip
Do not bend the hip more than 90 degrees
Do not cross legs or feet
Do not roll or lie on your unoperated side for the first 6 weeks
Do not twist the upper body when standing
Sleep on the back for the first 6 weeks
The patient may benefit from a shower chair or elevated seat for home use
Avoid bathing for 8 to 12 weeks (flexed and bent down in the tub)
Use aids to put on underwear/socks/shoes for 6 weeks to avoid deep hip flexion
TLSO brace when out of bed at all times, ok to apply sitting on edge of bed.
Otherwise, gradual return to normal activity as tolerated.
Maintain post operative splint clean and dry. Reinforce dressing if needed. Do not remove
splint until follow up with Orthopedic team.
Spear, Linda M (MRN 0000333437) Printed by Rosalie Aquino, RN [28092] at 9/27/2025 8:27 AM
Follow up in 2 weeks with Dr. Gerald M Sylvain, MD
Follow up in clinic in 3 months with Dr. Forage
Follow up with your primary care physician in 1-2 weeks.
Okay to resume previous home medications.
Please take any newly prescribed medications as directed on the bottle.
You can also take over-the-counter acetaminophen and ibuprofen for pain control unless
there is contraindications or told not to by your doctor. Do not take more than 4000 mg of
acetaminophen per day (be aware some of the prescribed narcotic medications do contained
Please go to the emergency room if you experience:
dizziness, confusion, or loss of consciousness
persistent fever, chills, nausea, or vomiting
increasing or expanding redness or severe swelling around incisions/wounds (signs of
any concern that you believe needs immediate medical attention
If you have any problems obtaining prescriptions at the pharmacy, please call the Trauma
team at 702-383-3969 immediately so we can try and resolve the issue.
Linda M Spear discharge to acute rehab facility.

Chief Complaint / Nature of Presenting Problem:
Patient is a 74-year-old female with a past medical history of hypertension, diabetes, atrial fibrillation, chronic pain syndrome and
debility who suffered a ground-level fall resulting in a fracture of the neck of the left femur. Patient underwent a left hip
hemiarthroplasty, patient is to follow-up with Dr. Gerald Sylvain in 2 weeks.. Patient also was found to have a T11 compression
fracture and a TLSO brace was recommended when out of bed. Patient is to follow-up with Dr. Farage for the compression fracture in
3 months. Patient had no postoperative complications and began working with physical therapy on day 3. Patient's pain was brought
under control she was evaluated by therapy and it was felt that she would benefit from a course of inpatient skilled therapy patient is

The patient is a 74-year-old female with a history of type 1 diabetes mellitus (A1c 8.8), hypertension, atrial fibrillation, chronic pain
syndrome, immunodeficiency, cognitive and communication deficits, debility, and generalized muscle weakness. She is status post
left hip hemiarthroplasty following a femoral neck fracture and is currently residing in a skilled nursing facility for continued
On current wound care evaluation, the left hip surgical incision appears stable, and continues to improve with Betadine dry dressing.
The sacral pressure ulcer is a stage 4 wound presents with 100% healthy granulation tissue with small serosanguineous drainage, no
odor, and no purulent discharge. The wound remains stable with healthy granulation but has reached a plateau despite consistent
advanced wound care, including collagen calcium alginate dressing three times weekly, offloading, moisture control, and nutritional
Due to the chronicity and stalled progression of the sacral pressure ulcer, and considering the patient?s significant comorbidities that
impair natural wound healing, the use of a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier is medically appropriate. The wound has not
demonstrated sufficient reduction in size or depth despite several weeks of comprehensive management. The application of a skin
substitute wound covering/skin barrier is indicated to promote epithelialization, encourage wound closure, and provide a physiological
environment that supports healing while protecting against fluid loss, shear, and external contamination.
The patient?s underlying diabetes, immunodeficiency, and overall debility compromise her body?s ability to generate new tissue and
repair the wound effectively. Continued conservative measures alone are unlikely to achieve timely closure. A skin substitute wound
covering/skin barrier will enhance the healing process by supporting tissue regeneration and providing a stable, protective barrier to
Radiographic evaluation on October 7, 2025, revealed no evidence of osteomyelitis. The wound bed is healthy and fully granulated,
indicating appropriate readiness for a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier. The patient continues on a high-protein diet and
pressure-relief regimen, both essential to promote healing and optimize outcomes.
In accordance with clinical practice guidelines, a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier is appropriate when a chronic wound fails
to show measurable improvement after four or more weeks of standard care. The plan is to proceed with evaluation for and
application of a skin substitute wound covering/skin barrier to the sacral wound ulcer to provide protection from the surrounding
environment, prevent deterioration, and improve healing outcomes.
